questions.question: What is REST and what are its core principles?
questions.answer:
REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style for building networked services. It's not a protocol — it's a set of constraints that, when applied, make APIs predictable and scalable.

Core constraints:

  • Stateless — each request contains all information needed to process it. Server holds no client session state. This is why we use JWT tokens (client sends token on every request) instead of server-side sessions.
  • Client-Server — UI and data storage are separated. They evolve independently.
  • Cacheable — responses should declare themselves cacheable or not. GET requests should be idempotent and cacheable.
  • Uniform Interface — consistent URL conventions, HTTP verbs, status codes. Resources are identified by URIs. Representations (JSON/XML) are transferred, not the resource itself.
  • Layered System — client doesn't know if it's talking directly to the server or through proxies/load balancers.

In FolioTrack's ASP.NET Core 10 API, every controller action maps to a resource + verb. GET /api/portfolios/{id} returns a portfolio representation. The client never knows whether a cache or the actual controller handled it.
followUps: What makes an API RESTful vs REST-like?
What is HATEOAS?
REST vs GraphQL vs gRPC?

followUpAnswers.What makes an API RESTful vs REST-like?:
A truly RESTful API satisfies all six REST constraints simultaneously. A REST-like (also called REST-ish or pseudo-REST) API uses HTTP verbs and JSON but violates one or more constraints.

Common violations that make an API REST-like, not RESTful:

  • Stateful server sessions — storing session data server-side instead of requiring the client to send all context (e.g., using ASP.NET session state with cookie auth).
  • No uniform interface — using verbs in URLs (POST /api/getUser, POST /api/deleteInvoice) rather than nouns + HTTP verbs.
  • Ignoring HTTP semantics — always returning 200 OK even for errors, putting error info in the body.
  • No HATEOAS — most production APIs skip this 6th constraint entirely. Roy Fielding (REST's creator) argues this makes them not truly REST, but the industry largely accepts this omission.

Practical answer: Most APIs called REST are actually REST-like. They use HTTP correctly (verbs, status codes, JSON) but skip HATEOAS and sometimes bend statelessness. That's fine for most use cases — but knowing the distinction shows architectural depth.
followUpAnswers.What is HATEOAS?:
HATEOAS (Hypermedia As The Engine Of Application State) is the sixth and most controversial REST constraint. It means API responses include links describing what the client can do next, rather than the client hardcoding URLs.

Example without HATEOAS:

// Response to GET /api/portfolios/42
{
  "id": 42,
  "name": "Growth Portfolio",
  "balance": 150000.00
}

Example with HATEOAS:

{
  "id": 42,
  "name": "Growth Portfolio",
  "balance": 150000.00,
  "links": [
    { "rel": "self",     "href": "/api/portfolios/42",          "method": "GET" },
    { "rel": "holdings", "href": "/api/portfolios/42/holdings",  "method": "GET" },
    { "rel": "update",  "href": "/api/portfolios/42",          "method": "PUT" },
    { "rel": "delete",  "href": "/api/portfolios/42",          "method": "DELETE" }
  ]
}

The client discovers available actions from the response rather than from out-of-band documentation. This enables server-side URL changes without breaking clients.

Real-world adoption: Almost never implemented in practice. The complexity is significant and most API consumers are typed clients (mobile apps, SPAs) that hardcode URLs anyway. GitHub's and PayPal's APIs implement partial HATEOAS. Most teams skip it and compensate with good OpenAPI docs.
followUpAnswers.REST vs GraphQL vs gRPC?:
These are three different API paradigms optimized for different use cases:

AspectRESTGraphQLgRPC
ProtocolHTTP/1.1 or 2HTTP (usually POST)HTTP/2
Data formatJSON / XMLJSONProtocol Buffers (binary)
TypingLoose (schema optional)Strongly typed schemaStrongly typed .proto
FetchingFixed response shapeClient defines exactly what fields it needsFixed message types
PerformanceGoodGood (avoids over-fetching)Excellent (binary, streaming)
Best forPublic APIs, CRUDComplex UIs, mobile (bandwidth)Internal microservices, streaming

GraphQL advantage: Eliminates over-fetching and under-fetching. A mobile app can request only name and balance instead of the full portfolio object.

gRPC advantage: Protocol Buffers are 5–10x smaller than JSON and faster to serialize. Ideal for internal service-to-service calls where you control both ends.

My context: FolioTrack uses REST because it's a public-facing API consumed by a browser SPA. For internal microservice communication in CS_PM_Service, gRPC would be my first choice for performance-critical calls.

questions.tip: Statelessness is the most important constraint — and the most commonly violated. Server-side sessions break horizontal scaling. JWT moves state to the client, which is why it's the standard in .NET APIs.

questions.question: What is the difference between GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE HTTP verbs?
questions.answer:
HTTP verbs define the intended operation on a resource:

  • GET — retrieve a resource. Safe (read-only) + idempotent (same result every time). Response should be cacheable.
  • POST — create a new resource or trigger an action. Not idempotent — calling twice creates two resources.
  • PUT — full replacement of a resource. Idempotent — sending the same PUT twice has the same effect. Client provides the complete new representation.
  • PATCH — partial update. Only the fields provided are changed. More efficient than PUT for large objects. Not inherently idempotent (depends on implementation).
  • DELETE — remove a resource. Idempotent — deleting an already-deleted resource returns 404 but has no further effect.

In FolioTrack's API: POST /api/portfolios creates, GET /api/portfolios/{id} reads, PUT /api/portfolios/{id} fully updates, PATCH /api/portfolios/{id} updates just the name, DELETE /api/portfolios/{id} removes.
followUps: What does idempotent mean?
When would you use PATCH over PUT?
What is a safe method?

followUpAnswers.What does idempotent mean?:
Idempotent means calling the operation multiple times produces the same result as calling it once. The server state ends up identical regardless of how many times you repeat the request.

Idempotent HTTP methods: GET, PUT, DELETE, HEAD, OPTIONS

Non-idempotent: POST (creates a new resource each time)

Concrete examples:

  • PUT /api/portfolios/42 with body { "name": "Growth" } — call it 1 or 100 times, the portfolio name is still "Growth". Same final state.
  • DELETE /api/portfolios/42 — first call deletes it (204), second call returns 404, but the state (portfolio is gone) is the same.
  • POST /api/portfolios — each call creates a new portfolio. 3 calls = 3 portfolios. Not idempotent.

Why it matters: Network failures and retries. If a DELETE request times out, you can safely retry it because the result is idempotent. With POST, a timeout means you don't know if the resource was created — retrying might create a duplicate. This is why payment APIs use idempotency keys to make POST effectively idempotent.
followUpAnswers.When would you use PATCH over PUT?:
Use PATCH when the client only needs to change a subset of fields on a large resource. Use PUT when the client is providing a complete replacement of the resource.

Concrete scenario — user profile:

// User resource has 20 fields: name, email, avatar, preferences, address, etc.

// PUT — client must send ALL fields every time
// Risky: if client doesn't know current values, it overwrites them with nulls
PUT /api/users/42
{
  "displayName": "Darshan",
  "email": "d@example.com",
  "avatarUrl": null,         // accidentally cleared!
  "theme": "dark",
  ... all 20 fields
}

// PATCH — client sends only what changed
PATCH /api/users/42
{ "displayName": "Darshan R" }  // only the name changes

ASP.NET Core PATCH with JsonPatchDocument (RFC 6902):

// Install: Microsoft.AspNetCore.JsonPatch + Microsoft.AspNetCore.Mvc.NewtonsoftJson
[HttpPatch("{id}")]
public async Task<IActionResult> Patch(int id,
    [FromBody] JsonPatchDocument<UpdateUserRequest> patch)
{
    var user = await _repo.GetAsync(id);
    if (user == null) return NotFound();
    patch.ApplyTo(user, ModelState);
    if (!ModelState.IsValid) return BadRequest(ModelState);
    await _repo.SaveAsync();
    return Ok(user);
}

Rule of thumb: If your client (mobile app, SPA) only has a partial view of the resource, use PATCH. If you're sending a full object anyway (e.g., a form that shows all fields), PUT is fine.
followUpAnswers.What is a safe method?:
A safe HTTP method is one that does not modify server state — it is purely read-only. Safe methods can be freely cached, prefetched, and retried without side effects.

Safe methods: GET, HEAD, OPTIONS

Unsafe methods: POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE (all modify state)

Important distinction from idempotent:

MethodSafe?Idempotent?
GETYesYes
HEADYesYes
OPTIONSYesYes
PUTNoYes
DELETENoYes
POSTNoNo
PATCHNoNo (typically)

Why it matters: Browsers, CDNs, and HTTP proxies may automatically retry or cache safe requests. A browser might prefetch GET links without user interaction. This is why you must never perform destructive operations on GET endpoints — don't do GET /api/users/42/delete.

In ASP.NET Core, the [HttpGet] attribute implicitly signals the endpoint is safe and idempotent. Search engine crawlers and link preloaders rely on this contract.

questions.tip: PATCH is underused but important for performance — updating a user's display name shouldn't require sending their entire profile. In .NET, use JsonPatchDocument<T> from Microsoft.AspNetCore.JsonPatch for RFC 6902 compliant PATCH.

questions.question: What are common HTTP status codes and what do they mean?
questions.answer:
Status codes tell the client what happened. They're grouped by first digit:

2xx Success:

  • 200 OK — standard success. GET, PUT responses.
  • 201 Created — resource was created. POST responses should return 201 + Location header pointing to the new resource.
  • 204 No Content — success but no body to return. DELETE and sometimes PUT.

3xx Redirection:

  • 301 Moved Permanently — resource moved; update bookmarks.
  • 302 Found — temporary redirect.

4xx Client Error:

  • 400 Bad Request — invalid input (validation failure, malformed JSON).
  • 401 Unauthorized — not authenticated (missing/invalid token).
  • 403 Forbidden — authenticated but not authorized for this resource.
  • 404 Not Found — resource doesn't exist.
  • 409 Conflict — state conflict (e.g., duplicate email).
  • 422 Unprocessable Entity — syntactically valid but semantically wrong.

5xx Server Error:

  • 500 Internal Server Error — unhandled exception.
  • 503 Service Unavailable — overloaded or down for maintenance.
followUps: What's the difference between 401 and 403?
When do you use 422 vs 400?

followUpAnswers.What's the difference between 401 and 40:
This is one of the most common confusions in API design. The names are misleading — the HTTP spec uses "Unauthorized" for 401 but means unauthenticated.

401 Unauthorized403 Forbidden
MeaningNot authenticated — the server doesn't know who you areAuthenticated but not authorized — the server knows who you are but you can't do this
TriggerMissing, expired, or invalid JWT/cookieValid token, but the user lacks the required role or ownership
Client actionLog in / refresh token and retryDon't retry — permissions must change first
ExampleAccessing /api/portfolios without a Bearer tokenAuthenticated as a regular user trying to hit /api/admin/users

ASP.NET Core behavior:

  • [Authorize] on a controller returns 401 when no valid auth token is present
  • [Authorize(Roles = "Admin")] returns 403 when the user is authenticated but not in the Admin role

Security note: Sometimes APIs deliberately return 404 instead of 403 to avoid leaking that a resource exists. For example, GET /api/portfolios/99 where portfolio 99 belongs to another user — returning 403 reveals the ID is valid. Returning 404 hides this. This is called "security through obscurity" and is a judgment call.
followUpAnswers.When do you use 422 vs 400?:
Both indicate the client sent bad data, but the distinction is about where the validation fails:

400 Bad Request — the request is malformed at the syntax/structure level. The server couldn't even parse or bind it properly.

  • Malformed JSON body ({ name: darshan } — missing quotes)
  • Wrong Content-Type header
  • Required route parameter missing
  • Type mismatch (string where int expected)

422 Unprocessable Entity — the request is syntactically valid and parseable, but semantically incorrect. The server understands the request but can't process it due to business rule violations.

  • Valid JSON but amount: -500 (negative invoice amount violates business rules)
  • Valid format but startDate is after endDate
  • Valid email format but email already registered in the system
// 400 — model binding failure (ASP.NET Core does this automatically with [ApiController])
// POST /api/invoices with body: { "amount": "not-a-number" }
// Returns 400 automatically

// 422 — business rule failure
[HttpPost]
public async Task<IActionResult> Create(CreateInvoiceRequest req)
{
    // Syntactically valid, but business rule violation
    if (req.DueDate < DateTime.Today)
        return UnprocessableEntity(new ProblemDetails
        {
            Status = 422,
            Title = "Invalid due date",
            Detail = "Due date cannot be in the past"
        });
}

Practical guidance: Many APIs use 400 for everything and that's acceptable. Using 422 for semantic errors is more precise and helps API consumers distinguish "fix your JSON" from "fix your business logic".

questions.tip: 401 vs 403: 401 = "I don't know who you are" (unauthenticated). 403 = "I know who you are but you can't do this" (unauthorized). Mixing these up is the most common REST API mistake.

questions.question: How does routing work in ASP.NET Core Web API?
questions.answer:
ASP.NET Core routing matches incoming URLs to controller actions. Two main approaches:

Attribute routing (preferred for APIs):

[ApiController]
[Route("api/[controller]")]  // api/portfolios
public class PortfoliosController : ControllerBase
{
    [HttpGet]                          // GET api/portfolios
    [HttpGet("{id:int}")]              // GET api/portfolios/42
    [HttpGet("{id}/holdings")]         // GET api/portfolios/42/holdings
    [HttpPost]                         // POST api/portfolios
    [HttpPut("{id}")]                  // PUT api/portfolios/42
    [HttpDelete("{id}")]               // DELETE api/portfolios/42
}

Route constraints ({id:int}, {id:guid}, {id:alpha}) — validate the route parameter type before matching. A request to api/portfolios/abc won't match {id:int} — returns 404 instead of a bad cast exception in the action.

Conventional routing (from app.MapControllerRoute) is typically used for MVC views, not APIs.

projectExample.explanation: FolioTrack used versioned routing via Asp.Versioning NuGet package — the version segment is part of the URL.

followUps: What are route constraints?
How do you handle route conflicts?
What is endpoint routing?

followUpAnswers.What are route constraints?:
Route constraints restrict what values a route parameter will match. They are evaluated before the action executes — a request that doesn't satisfy the constraint causes that route to not match (404), not a runtime error inside the action.

Built-in constraints:

ConstraintExampleMatches
int{id:int}Any integer: 42, -1
guid{id:guid}GUIDs: 3fa85f64-5717-...
alpha{name:alpha}Letters only: growth
minlength(n){slug:minlength(3)}Min 3 chars
range(min,max){page:range(1,100)}Integer in range
regex(expr){code:regex(^[A-Z]{{3}}$)}Regex match
// Only matches numeric IDs — /api/portfolios/abc returns 404
[HttpGet("{id:int}")]
public async Task<IActionResult> Get(int id) { }

// Custom constraint — register in Program.cs
public class SlugConstraint : IRouteConstraint
{
    public bool Match(HttpContext? ctx, IRouter? route, string routeKey,
        RouteValueDictionary values, RouteDirection direction)
    {
        var value = values[routeKey]?.ToString();
        return value != null && Regex.IsMatch(value, @"^[a-z0-9-]+$");
    }
}

Register custom constraint: builder.Services.Configure<RouteOptions>(o => o.ConstraintMap["slug"] = typeof(SlugConstraint))
followUpAnswers.How do you handle route conflicts?:
Route conflicts occur when two routes could match the same URL. ASP.NET Core resolves ambiguity in predictable ways, but some cases throw an AmbiguousMatchException at runtime.

How ASP.NET Core resolves conflicts:

  • More specific route wins — {id:int} beats {id} because the constraint narrows the match
  • Literal segments beat parameters — api/portfolios/featured beats api/portfolios/{id}
  • Order matters for equally specific routes — first registered wins in conventional routing
// Conflict: both could match GET /api/portfolios/featured
[HttpGet("{id}")]              // would match 'featured' as a string
[HttpGet("featured")]         // literal — wins over parameterized route

// Resolution: ASP.NET Core prefers literal 'featured'
// To be explicit, use constraints:
[HttpGet("{id:int}")]          // only matches integers, so 'featured' goes to other route
[HttpGet("featured")]         // matches string 'featured'

When you get AmbiguousMatchException:

  • Two actions have identical route templates and the same HTTP method
  • Fix: add a constraint, rename the route, or use [HttpGet("{id:int}")] vs [HttpGet("{slug:alpha}")]

Order attribute: Use [Route("...", Order = 1)] to explicitly control match priority when other mechanisms don't resolve the conflict.
followUpAnswers.What is endpoint routing?:
Endpoint routing (introduced in ASP.NET Core 3.0) decouples route matching from endpoint execution. The router runs early in the pipeline and stores the matched endpoint in HttpContext, so middleware can inspect routing decisions before the endpoint executes.

Before endpoint routing (ASP.NET Core 2.x): Routing and execution were coupled — middleware couldn't know which action would handle a request without executing it.

With endpoint routing:

// Program.cs — order matters
app.UseRouting();           // 1. Match request to an endpoint, store in HttpContext
app.UseAuthentication();    // 2. Can now inspect matched endpoint metadata
app.UseAuthorization();     // 3. Reads [Authorize] from matched endpoint
app.MapControllers();       // 4. Execute the matched endpoint

Why it matters:

  • UseAuthorization() can read [Authorize] attributes from the matched endpoint before executing it — this is what enables policy-based authorization to work correctly
  • Middleware can access context.GetEndpoint() to make decisions based on which endpoint will run
  • Rate limiting, CORS, and other middleware can apply per-endpoint policies by reading endpoint metadata
// Middleware that checks if matched endpoint has [Authorize]
app.Use(async (context, next) =>
{
    var endpoint = context.GetEndpoint();
    var hasAuth = endpoint?.Metadata.GetMetadata<IAuthorizeData>() != null;
    // make a decision before execution
    await next();
});

questions.tip: [ApiController] attribute changes several default behaviors: automatic 400 on model validation failure, infers [FromBody] for complex types, requires attribute routing. Always use it on API controllers.

questions.question: What is model binding in ASP.NET Core and how does it work?
questions.answer:
Model binding automatically maps HTTP request data to action method parameters. ASP.NET Core looks in these locations in order:

  • [FromRoute] — URL route segments (/api/products/{id})
  • [FromQuery] — query string (?page=1&size=20)
  • [FromBody] — request body (typically JSON). Only one [FromBody] parameter per action.
  • [FromHeader] — HTTP header
  • [FromForm] — form data (multipart/form-data for file uploads)
  • [FromServices] — resolved from DI container
// Complex type from body automatically with [ApiController]
public async Task<IActionResult> Create([FromBody] CreatePortfolioRequest request) { }

// Multiple sources in one action
public async Task<IActionResult> Update(
    [FromRoute] int id,
    [FromBody] UpdateRequest request,
    [FromQuery] bool notifyUser = false) { }

[ApiController] inference: With [ApiController], complex types default to [FromBody] and simple types default to [FromQuery] — so you often don't need explicit attributes.
followUps: What happens when model binding fails?
How do you bind a file upload?
What is custom model binding?

followUpAnswers.What happens when model binding fails?:
When model binding fails, ASP.NET Core adds errors to ModelState. What happens next depends on whether [ApiController] is applied:

With [ApiController] (standard for APIs): The framework automatically returns a 400 Bad Request with a ValidationProblemDetails JSON body before your action method even runs. You never see the call.

// POST /api/portfolios with body: { "id": "not-a-number" }
// With [ApiController], this never reaches the action:
// Response: 400
// Body:
// {
//   "type": "https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc9110#section-15.5.1",
//   "title": "One or more validation errors occurred.",
//   "status": 400,
//   "errors": {
//     "id": ["The value 'not-a-number' is not valid for Int32."]
//   }
// }

Without [ApiController]: The action runs, and you must check ModelState.IsValid manually:

[HttpPost]
public IActionResult Create(CreateRequest request)
{
    if (!ModelState.IsValid)
        return BadRequest(ModelState);
    // ...
}

Customizing the 400 response:

// Override the default behavior in Program.cs
builder.Services.Configure<ApiBehaviorOptions>(options =>
{
    options.InvalidModelStateResponseFactory = context =>
    {
        var errors = context.ModelState
            .Where(e => e.Value!.Errors.Count > 0)
            .ToDictionary(k => k.Key,
                v => v.Value!.Errors.Select(e => e.ErrorMessage));
        return new BadRequestObjectResult(new { errors });
    };
});
followUpAnswers.How do you bind a file upload?:
File uploads use [FromForm] with IFormFile (single file) or IFormFileCollection / List<IFormFile> (multiple files). The request must be multipart/form-data.

// Single file
[HttpPost("upload")]
[Consumes("multipart/form-data")]
public async Task<IActionResult> Upload([FromForm] IFormFile file)
{
    if (file == null || file.Length == 0)
        return BadRequest("No file provided");

    // Validate extension
    var ext = Path.GetExtension(file.FileName).ToLower();
    if (ext is not ".pdf" and not ".jpg" and not ".png")
        return BadRequest("Unsupported file type");

    // Validate size (10 MB max)
    if (file.Length > 10 * 1024 * 1024)
        return BadRequest("File too large");

    var path = Path.Combine("uploads", Guid.NewGuid() + ext);
    await using var stream = System.IO.File.Create(path);
    await file.CopyToAsync(stream);

    return Ok(new { path });
}

// Multiple files + form fields together
public record PortfolioImportRequest(
    [FromForm] IFormFile CsvFile,
    [FromForm] string PortfolioName,
    [FromForm] bool Overwrite);

[HttpPost("import")]
[Consumes("multipart/form-data")]
public async Task<IActionResult> Import([FromForm] PortfolioImportRequest req) { }

Size limits: Configure in Program.cs to allow large uploads:

builder.Services.Configure<FormOptions>(o => o.MultipartBodyLengthLimit = 50_000_000); // 50 MB
followUpAnswers.What is custom model binding?:
Custom model binding lets you control how ASP.NET Core converts raw request data into a parameter type. Implement IModelBinder and register it via IModelBinderProvider or the [ModelBinder] attribute.

Common use cases:

  • Comma-separated query params to List<int>
  • Trimming/normalizing string inputs
  • Converting a string to a domain value object (e.g., "USD" → Currency.USD)
// Bind comma-separated IDs: GET /api/portfolios?ids=1,2,3
public class CommaDelimitedArrayModelBinder : IModelBinder
{
    public Task BindModelAsync(ModelBindingContext context)
    {
        var value = context.ValueProvider.GetValue(context.ModelName).FirstValue;
        if (string.IsNullOrEmpty(value))
        {
            context.Result = ModelBindingResult.Success(Array.Empty<int>());
            return Task.CompletedTask;
        }

        var ids = value.Split(',', StringSplitOptions.RemoveEmptyEntries)
            .Select(s => int.TryParse(s.Trim(), out var id) ? id : -1)
            .Where(id => id > 0)
            .ToArray();

        context.Result = ModelBindingResult.Success(ids);
        return Task.CompletedTask;
    }
}

// Usage on action parameter
[HttpGet]
public async Task<IActionResult> GetMany(
    [ModelBinder(typeof(CommaDelimitedArrayModelBinder))] int[] ids) { }

// Or register globally via provider
builder.Services.AddControllers(o =>
    o.ModelBinderProviders.Insert(0, new CommaDelimitedArrayBinderProvider()));

questions.tip: Custom model binder — useful for binding comma-separated query params to a List, or converting a string to a domain type. Implement IModelBinder and register with [ModelBinder(typeof(MyBinder))].

questions.question: What is model validation in ASP.NET Core and how do you use it?
questions.answer:
Model validation ensures incoming data meets requirements before the action body executes. Three layers:

1. Data Annotations (on the model):

public class CreateInvoiceRequest
{
    [Required]
    [StringLength(200)]
    public string ClientName { get; set; } = string.Empty;

    [Range(0.01, 1_000_000)]
    public decimal Amount { get; set; }

    [EmailAddress]
    public string? ClientEmail { get; set; }

    [RegularExpression(@"^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$")]
    public string DueDate { get; set; } = string.Empty;
}

2. Automatic 400 response with [ApiController]: If ModelState.IsValid is false, ASP.NET Core returns a 400 ProblemDetails response automatically — no if (!ModelState.IsValid) return BadRequest(...) needed.

3. FluentValidation (library) — for complex, cross-field validation logic that data annotations can't express. Supports async validation (e.g., check if email already exists in DB).
followUps: What is IValidatableObject?
FluentValidation vs data annotations — when to use which?

followUpAnswers.What is IValidatableObject?:
IValidatableObject is a .NET interface that lets a model class validate itself with cross-field logic — it's the built-in alternative to FluentValidation for cross-property rules without adding a library.

The Validate method is called by ASP.NET Core's model validation pipeline after data annotations pass.

public class CreateBookingRequest : IValidatableObject
{
    [Required]
    public DateTime StartDate { get; set; }

    [Required]
    public DateTime EndDate { get; set; }

    [Range(1, 20)]
    public int GuestCount { get; set; }

    public string? SpecialRequest { get; set; }

    public IEnumerable<ValidationResult> Validate(ValidationContext context)
    {
        // Cross-field rule 1: end must be after start
        if (EndDate <= StartDate)
            yield return new ValidationResult(
                "End date must be after start date",
                new[] { nameof(EndDate) });

        // Cross-field rule 2: special requests need more time
        if (!string.IsNullOrEmpty(SpecialRequest)
            && (StartDate - DateTime.Today).TotalDays < 7)
            yield return new ValidationResult(
                "Special requests require at least 7 days notice",
                new[] { nameof(SpecialRequest) });
    }
}

Limitation: IValidatableObject.Validate is synchronous — you cannot make DB calls (check email uniqueness etc.) inside it. For async validation, you need FluentValidation.
followUpAnswers.FluentValidation vs data annotations — w:
Both validate input, but they serve different purposes and have different capabilities:

AspectData AnnotationsFluentValidation
SetupAttributes on the model classSeparate validator class
Async supportNoYes (MustAsync)
Cross-field rulesLimited (IValidatableObject)First-class support
DB/service callsNoYes (inject services)
ReuseTied to the modelValidator is a separate class, composable
TestingHard to unit testEasy to unit test in isolation
Complex conditionsNot supportedWhen(), Unless(), WithMessage()
// FluentValidation example — async rule with DB check
public class CreateUserRequestValidator : AbstractValidator<CreateUserRequest>
{
    private readonly IUserRepository _repo;

    public CreateUserRequestValidator(IUserRepository repo)
    {
        _repo = repo;

        RuleFor(x => x.Email)
            .NotEmpty()
            .EmailAddress()
            .MustAsync(async (email, ct) =>
                !await _repo.EmailExistsAsync(email))
            .WithMessage("Email is already registered");

        RuleFor(x => x.Password)
            .MinimumLength(8)
            .Matches(@"[A-Z]")
            .WithMessage("Must contain an uppercase letter");

        RuleFor(x => x.ConfirmPassword)
            .Equal(x => x.Password)
            .WithMessage("Passwords do not match");
    }
}

My approach: Use data annotations for simple format rules ([Required], [EmailAddress], [StringLength]). Use FluentValidation for anything cross-field, async, or that requires a service. Register with builder.Services.AddFluentValidationAutoValidation().

questions.tip: Always use FluentValidation for business rule validation ("email must be unique", "amount must be less than client's credit limit") — these need async DB calls which data annotations can't do. Use data annotations for simple format rules ([Required], [StringLength]).

questions.question: What is CORS and how do you configure it in ASP.NET Core?
questions.answer:
CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) is a browser security mechanism that blocks a web page from making requests to a different origin (domain/port/protocol) than the one that served the page.

When FolioTrack's Angular frontend on localhost:4200 calls the API on localhost:5001, the browser sends an Origin: http://localhost:4200 header. The API must respond with Access-Control-Allow-Origin: http://localhost:4200 (or *) or the browser blocks the response.

// Program.cs
builder.Services.AddCors(options =>
{
    options.AddPolicy("FolioTrackCors", policy =>
    {
        policy.WithOrigins(
                "http://localhost:4200",   // dev
                "https://foliotrack.app")  // prod
            .AllowAnyHeader()
            .AllowAnyMethod()
            .AllowCredentials();  // required for cookies/auth
    });
});

// Middleware — BEFORE routing
app.UseCors("FolioTrackCors");
app.UseAuthentication();
app.UseAuthorization();

Preflight requests: Before non-simple requests (POST with JSON body), the browser sends an HTTP OPTIONS request to check CORS. ASP.NET Core handles this automatically when CORS middleware is configured.
followUps: What is a preflight request?
Why is CORS a browser-only restriction?
What's the difference between simple and non-simple requests?

followUpAnswers.What is a preflight request?:
A preflight request is an HTTP OPTIONS request the browser automatically sends before a non-simple cross-origin request. It asks the server "are you willing to accept this kind of request from this origin?" before committing to the actual request.

The preflight exchange:

// 1. Browser sends preflight automatically — you don't control this
OPTIONS /api/portfolios HTTP/1.1
Origin: http://localhost:4200
Access-Control-Request-Method: POST
Access-Control-Request-Headers: Content-Type, Authorization

// 2. Server must respond with permission headers
HTTP/1.1 204 No Content
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: http://localhost:4200
Access-Control-Allow-Methods: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE
Access-Control-Allow-Headers: Content-Type, Authorization
Access-Control-Max-Age: 86400  // browser caches this for 24h

// 3. Browser sees approval and sends the actual POST request

ASP.NET Core handles preflight automatically when you configure CORS middleware correctly. You don't need to write an OPTIONS action.

Performance note: Preflights add a round trip before every non-simple request. Use Access-Control-Max-Age to tell browsers to cache the preflight result (default is low — setting 86400 seconds means browsers only preflight once per day per endpoint).
followUpAnswers.Why is CORS a browser-only restriction?:
CORS is enforced exclusively by the browser as a security policy protecting end users — not as a server security mechanism. The server simply adds HTTP headers; the browser decides whether to expose the response to JavaScript.

What actually happens:

  1. Browser sends the request (with the Origin header added automatically)
  2. Server processes the request and returns the response (always)
  3. Browser checks response headers — if Access-Control-Allow-Origin doesn't match, the browser hides the response from your JavaScript

The response was already received by the browser — CORS just controls whether your JavaScript code can see it.

Proof that it's browser-only:

  • curl -X POST https://api.example.com/data — works fine, no CORS. curl doesn't enforce it.
  • Postman — works fine, no CORS. Tools ignore it.
  • Server-to-server calls (.NET HttpClient) — completely unaffected by CORS.
  • Browser JavaScript — blocked if headers are wrong.

Security implication: CORS does NOT protect your API from unauthorized server-side callers. For that, you need authentication (JWT/OAuth) + authorization. CORS only protects browser users from malicious scripts on other websites reading their data from your API.
followUpAnswers.What's the difference between simple and:
The CORS spec distinguishes simple requests (no preflight needed) from preflighted requests based on specific criteria.

Simple requests (no OPTIONS preflight) must meet ALL of these:

  • Method is GET, POST, or HEAD
  • Headers are only: Accept, Accept-Language, Content-Language, Content-Type
  • If Content-Type is used, it must be: application/x-www-form-urlencoded, multipart/form-data, or text/plain

Preflighted (non-simple) requests — anything outside the above:

  • POST with Content-Type: application/json — needs preflight (JSON is not in the simple list)
  • PUT, PATCH, DELETE — needs preflight (method not in simple list)
  • Any custom header (e.g., Authorization, X-Correlation-Id) — needs preflight

Practical impact in ASP.NET Core APIs:

  • Almost every real API request is non-simple because APIs use application/json and Authorization headers
  • All your POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE requests will trigger preflights
  • This is why properly configuring CORS in ASP.NET Core (including handling OPTIONS) is essential — a misconfigured CORS setup will break your Angular/React frontend entirely

questions.tip: CORS is enforced by the browser, not the server. A curl request or Postman call to an API with "wrong" CORS headers will succeed — only the browser enforces it. CORS is not a security mechanism for APIs; it's a browser protection for end users.

questions.question: What are action filters in ASP.NET Core Web API and how do you create a custom one?
questions.answer:
Action filters run before or after an action method executes. They implement IActionFilter (sync) or IAsyncActionFilter (async) and intercept the action pipeline.

Built-in filters: [Authorize], [ValidateAntiForgeryToken], [ResponseCache], [Produces]

Custom action filter example — execution timing:

public class ExecutionTimingFilter : IAsyncActionFilter
{
    private readonly ILogger<ExecutionTimingFilter> _logger;
    public ExecutionTimingFilter(ILogger<ExecutionTimingFilter> logger)
        => _logger = logger;

    public async Task OnActionExecutionAsync(
        ActionExecutingContext context, ActionExecutionDelegate next)
    {
        var sw = Stopwatch.StartNew();
        var result = await next();  // execute the action
        sw.Stop();

        if (sw.ElapsedMilliseconds > 500)
            _logger.LogWarning("{Action} took {ms}ms",
                context.ActionDescriptor.DisplayName, sw.ElapsedMilliseconds);
    }
}

// Register globally in Program.cs
builder.Services.AddControllers(options =>
    options.Filters.Add<ExecutionTimingFilter>());
followUps: What is the filter pipeline order?
Difference between action filter and middleware?
What is an exception filter?

followUpAnswers.What is the filter pipeline order?:
ASP.NET Core runs filters in a specific, predictable order. Each filter type has a defined stage in the pipeline, and within the same type, order is determined by Order property or scope (global → controller → action).

Filter execution order (outer to inner):

  1. Authorization Filters — run first. If denied, short-circuit everything else. ([Authorize])
  2. Resource Filters — wrap everything. Can short-circuit before model binding. Useful for caching.
  3. Model Binding — happens here (not a filter, but part of the pipeline)
  4. Action Filters — run immediately before and after the action method
  5. Action Method Executes
  6. Result Filters — run before and after the action result is executed (IActionResult.ExecuteResultAsync)
  7. Exception Filters — only run if an unhandled exception occurred in steps 2–5

Scope order within the same filter type: Global → Controller → Action (for OnActionExecuting). Reversed for OnActionExecuted (Action → Controller → Global).

// Control order explicitly
[ServiceFilter(typeof(MyFilter), Order = 1)]
[ServiceFilter(typeof(AnotherFilter), Order = 2)]
public class MyController : ControllerBase { }

Short-circuiting: Any filter can short-circuit by setting context.Result without calling next(). Authorization filters use this to return 401/403 early.
followUpAnswers.Difference between action filter and mid:
Both intercept the request pipeline, but they operate at different levels and have access to different context:

AspectMiddlewareAction Filter
LevelHTTP pipeline (raw)MVC pipeline (after routing + model binding)
ContextHttpContext onlyActionExecutingContext with controller, action, model state, parameters
ScopeAll requests (unless you write conditions)Specific controllers/actions via attributes
Model state accessNoYes
Action parametersNoYes (context.ActionArguments)
Result modificationCan only modify response streamCan replace the IActionResult before execution
DI injectionConstructor injection (middleware)Constructor injection (with ServiceFilter)

Use middleware for: Logging all HTTP traffic, authentication, CORS, rate limiting, compression — things that apply to every request or don't need MVC context.

Use action filters for: Per-controller/per-action logic that needs action arguments, ModelState, or ActionDescriptor — e.g., audit logging with the user's action name, response caching based on action metadata.
followUpAnswers.What is an exception filter?:
An exception filter implements IExceptionFilter or IAsyncExceptionFilter and handles unhandled exceptions that occur within the MVC filter pipeline (action filters, action execution, result filters).

public class ApiExceptionFilter : IExceptionFilter
{
    private readonly ILogger<ApiExceptionFilter> _logger;

    public ApiExceptionFilter(ILogger<ApiExceptionFilter> logger)
        => _logger = logger;

    public void OnException(ExceptionContext context)
    {
        _logger.LogError(context.Exception,
            "Unhandled exception in {Action}",
            context.ActionDescriptor.DisplayName);

        var (status, title) = context.Exception switch
        {
            KeyNotFoundException => (404, "Not Found"),
            UnauthorizedAccessException => (403, "Forbidden"),
            ValidationException => (400, "Validation Error"),
            _ => (500, "Internal Server Error")
        };

        context.Result = new ObjectResult(new ProblemDetails
        {
            Status = status,
            Title = title,
            Detail = status < 500 ? context.Exception.Message : "An error occurred"
        })
        { StatusCode = status };

        context.ExceptionHandled = true; // prevents propagation
    }
}

// Register globally
builder.Services.AddControllers(o =>
    o.Filters.Add<ApiExceptionFilter>());

Exception filter vs exception handling middleware:

  • Exception filter — only catches exceptions from the MVC pipeline. Misses exceptions from middleware (e.g., routing errors).
  • Exception handling middleware (app.UseExceptionHandler()) — catches everything. Preferred for global error handling.
  • Use exception filters for MVC-specific exception mapping (e.g., convert domain exceptions to HTTP responses); use middleware as the outer safety net.

questions.tip: Filter vs Middleware: Middleware operates on the raw HTTP pipeline (HttpContext). Filters operate within the MVC pipeline and have access to controller/action context (ActionDescriptor, ModelState). Use filters when you need MVC-aware logic; use middleware for HTTP-level cross-cutting concerns.

questions.question: How do you implement API versioning in ASP.NET Core?
questions.answer:
API versioning lets you evolve your API without breaking existing clients. The Asp.Versioning.Mvc NuGet package (previously Microsoft.AspNetCore.Mvc.Versioning) handles this cleanly.

Three versioning strategies:

  • URL segment: /api/v1/portfolios — most visible, easy to test in browser, my preference.
  • Query string: /api/portfolios?api-version=1.0
  • Header: X-Api-Version: 1.0 — keeps URLs clean but harder to test
// Program.cs
builder.Services.AddApiVersioning(options =>
{
    options.DefaultApiVersion = new ApiVersion(1, 0);
    options.AssumeDefaultVersionWhenUnspecified = true;
    options.ReportApiVersions = true; // returns api-supported-versions header
}).AddMvc();

// Controller
[ApiController]
[ApiVersion("1.0")]
[ApiVersion("2.0")]
[Route("api/v{version:apiVersion}/portfolios")]
public class PortfoliosController : ControllerBase
{
    [HttpGet, MapToApiVersion("1.0")]
    public IActionResult GetV1() { /* old format */ }

    [HttpGet, MapToApiVersion("2.0")]
    public IActionResult GetV2() { /* new format */ }
}

projectExample.explanation: ServiceSelection project used URL segment versioning to add Angular Web Component-specific endpoints in v2 while maintaining the v1 REST API for backward compatibility.

followUps: How do you deprecate an API version?
How do you version Swagger/OpenAPI docs?

followUpAnswers.How do you deprecate an API version?:
Deprecation is a two-step process: mark the version as deprecated (so clients are warned) and eventually sunset it (remove it).

Step 1 — Mark as deprecated in code:

[ApiController]
[ApiVersion("1.0", Deprecated = true)]  // flags it as deprecated
[ApiVersion("2.0")]                       // current supported version
[Route("api/v{version:apiVersion}/portfolios")]
public class PortfoliosController : ControllerBase { }

Step 2 — The framework automatically adds response headers:

// ASP.NET Core Versioning adds these to every v1 response:
api-deprecated-versions: 1.0
api-supported-versions: 2.0
Sunset: Sat, 01 Jan 2026 00:00:00 GMT  // RFC 8594 header

Step 3 — Add Sunset header manually for a specific date:

// Custom action filter to add Sunset header to deprecated version responses
public class DeprecationHeaderFilter : IActionFilter
{
    public void OnActionExecuting(ActionExecutingContext context)
    {
        var version = context.HttpContext.GetRequestedApiVersion();
        if (version?.MajorVersion == 1)
        {
            context.HttpContext.Response.Headers
                .Append("Sunset", "Sat, 01 Jan 2026 00:00:00 GMT");
            context.HttpContext.Response.Headers
                .Append("Deprecation", "true");
        }
    }
    public void OnActionExecuted(ActionExecutedContext context) { }
}

Communication: Document deprecated versions in your OpenAPI/Swagger docs, add release notes, and give consumers a migration timeline (typically 6–12 months).
followUpAnswers.How do you version Swagger/OpenAPI docs?:
When you have multiple API versions, Swagger should generate a separate document for each version so clients can explore v1 and v2 independently.

// Program.cs — install: Asp.Versioning.Mvc.ApiExplorer + Swashbuckle
builder.Services.AddApiVersioning(options =>
{
    options.DefaultApiVersion = new ApiVersion(1, 0);
    options.AssumeDefaultVersionWhenUnspecified = true;
    options.ReportApiVersions = true;
}).AddApiExplorer(options =>
{
    // formats version as 'v1', 'v2' in Swagger groups
    options.GroupNameFormat = "'v'VVV";
    options.SubstituteApiVersionInUrl = true;
});

builder.Services.AddSwaggerGen(options =>
{
    // Create a Swagger doc per API version
    options.SwaggerDoc("v1", new OpenApiInfo
    {
        Title = "Portfolio API", Version = "v1",
        Description = "Version 1.0 — deprecated, use v2"
    });
    options.SwaggerDoc("v2", new OpenApiInfo
    {
        Title = "Portfolio API", Version = "v2"
    });
});

if (app.Environment.IsDevelopment())
{
    app.UseSwagger();
    app.UseSwaggerUI(options =>
    {
        // Both docs available in Swagger UI dropdown
        options.SwaggerEndpoint("/swagger/v1/swagger.json", "Portfolio API v1");
        options.SwaggerEndpoint("/swagger/v2/swagger.json", "Portfolio API v2");
    });
}

Result: Swagger UI shows a version dropdown. Each version's endpoints, schemas, and deprecation notes are displayed separately. Clients can generate type-safe clients from the specific version's OpenAPI JSON.

questions.tip: Always version from day one — retrofitting versioning is painful. Even if v1 and v2 are identical initially, the infrastructure is there. Mark deprecated versions with [Obsolete] and Deprecated = true in ApiVersionAttribute.

questions.question: How do you implement global exception handling in ASP.NET Core Web API?
questions.answer:
Never let unhandled exceptions bubble up to clients as 500 HTML error pages. Two main approaches:

1. Exception handling middleware (preferred for APIs):

// Custom middleware
app.UseExceptionHandler(errorApp =>
{
    errorApp.Run(async context =>
    {
        var feature = context.Features.Get<IExceptionHandlerFeature>()!;
        var ex = feature.Error;

        var statusCode = ex switch
        {
            KeyNotFoundException => 404,
            UnauthorizedAccessException => 403,
            ValidationException => 400,
            _ => 500
        };

        context.Response.StatusCode = statusCode;
        context.Response.ContentType = "application/problem+json";
        await context.Response.WriteAsJsonAsync(new ProblemDetails
        {
            Status = statusCode,
            Title = ex.GetType().Name,
            Detail = ex.Message  // don't expose stack trace in prod!
        });
    });
});

2. IExceptionHandler interface (.NET 8): Cleaner way — implement IExceptionHandler, register with builder.Services.AddExceptionHandler<MyHandler>(), then call app.UseExceptionHandler().
followUps: What is ProblemDetails (RFC 7807)?
How do you hide internal error details in production?

followUpAnswers.What is ProblemDetails (RFC 7807)?:
ProblemDetails is a standardized JSON error response format defined by RFC 7807 (now superseded by RFC 9457). It provides a machine-readable structure for API errors so clients can handle them programmatically rather than parsing free-form error strings.

Standard ProblemDetails properties:

// RFC 7807 JSON structure
{
  "type": "https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc9110#section-15.5.5",  // URI describing the error type
  "title": "Not Found",           // human-readable summary (doesn't change per instance)
  "status": 404,                  // HTTP status code
  "detail": "Portfolio 99 was not found",  // specific to this occurrence
  "instance": "/api/portfolios/99"         // URI of the request that caused it
}

In ASP.NET Core:

// [ApiController] returns ProblemDetails automatically for 400 and 404
// Custom ProblemDetails with extensions
return new ObjectResult(new ProblemDetails
{
    Status = 404,
    Title = "Portfolio Not Found",
    Detail = $"No portfolio with ID {id} exists for this account",
    Instance = HttpContext.Request.Path,
    Type = "https://myapi.com/errors/not-found"
}) { StatusCode = 404 };

// ValidationProblemDetails (for 400) extends ProblemDetails with:
// "errors": { "fieldName": ["error message"] }

.NET 8 ProblemDetails factory customization:

builder.Services.AddProblemDetails(options =>
{
    options.CustomizeProblemDetails = ctx =>
    {
        ctx.ProblemDetails.Extensions["correlationId"] =
            ctx.HttpContext.TraceIdentifier;
        ctx.ProblemDetails.Extensions["timestamp"] =
            DateTime.UtcNow;
    };
});
followUpAnswers.How do you hide internal error details i:
Exposing exception messages, stack traces, or internal paths in production is a security risk — it reveals implementation details that attackers can use for targeted exploits.

Strategy: environment-based detail level

// In IExceptionHandler or middleware:
public async ValueTask<bool> TryHandleAsync(
    HttpContext ctx, Exception ex, CancellationToken ct)
{
    _logger.LogError(ex, "Unhandled exception: {Message}", ex.Message);

    var isDev = _env.IsDevelopment();

    var problem = new ProblemDetails
    {
        Status = 500,
        Title = "Internal Server Error",
        // In dev: show actual message. In prod: generic message only.
        Detail = isDev
            ? ex.ToString()  // full stack trace in dev
            : "An unexpected error occurred. Use the trace ID to contact support.",
        Instance = ctx.Request.Path
    };

    // Always include trace ID — safe to expose, useful for support
    problem.Extensions["traceId"] = Activity.Current?.Id
        ?? ctx.TraceIdentifier;

    // Only include exception type in dev
    if (isDev)
        problem.Extensions["exceptionType"] = ex.GetType().Name;

    ctx.Response.StatusCode = 500;
    await ctx.Response.WriteAsJsonAsync(problem, ct);
    return true;
}

Rules:

  • Log the full exception server-side (Serilog, App Insights) — never lose the detail
  • Return only a trace ID to the client — support can look up the full error by trace ID
  • 4xx errors (client's fault) can include descriptive detail — the user needs to know what to fix
  • 5xx errors (server's fault) must be generic — internal details stay internal
  • Never expose: stack traces, internal paths, SQL queries, connection strings, assembly versions

questions.tip: Never return raw exception messages or stack traces in production. Log them internally (Serilog), return a generic ProblemDetails to the client. Distinguish between 4xx (client's fault, detailed message is OK) and 5xx (server's fault, generic message only).

questions.question: What is Swagger/OpenAPI and how do you set it up in ASP.NET Core?
questions.answer:
OpenAPI is a specification for describing REST APIs in machine-readable format (JSON/YAML). Swagger is the tooling ecosystem around OpenAPI — Swagger UI renders a browsable, interactive API documentation page.

In ASP.NET Core 8, Swagger support is built-in via Microsoft.AspNetCore.OpenApi. The full UI requires Swashbuckle.AspNetCore or Scalar:

// Program.cs
builder.Services.AddEndpointsApiExplorer();
builder.Services.AddSwaggerGen(options =>
{
    options.SwaggerDoc("v1", new OpenApiInfo
    {
        Title = "FolioTrack API", Version = "v1"
    });
    // Add JWT auth button in Swagger UI
    options.AddSecurityDefinition("Bearer", new OpenApiSecurityScheme
    {
        Type = SecuritySchemeType.Http,
        Scheme = "bearer",
        BearerFormat = "JWT"
    });
});

if (app.Environment.IsDevelopment())
{
    app.UseSwagger();
    app.UseSwaggerUI();
}

XML comments on controllers/actions appear in Swagger UI if you enable <GenerateDocumentationFile>true</GenerateDocumentationFile> in csproj.
followUps: What is the difference between Swashbuckle and NSwag?
How do you add Swagger for versioned APIs?

followUpAnswers.What is the difference between Swashbuck:
Both generate OpenAPI documentation from ASP.NET Core applications, but they have different strengths and approaches:

AspectSwashbuckleNSwag
Primary focusOpenAPI document generation + Swagger UIOpenAPI generation + client code generation
Client generationNeeds external toolingBuilt-in: generates C#, TypeScript clients
UISwagger UI (well-known)ReDoc or Swagger UI
Reflection approachASP.NET Core ApiExplorer + Swashbuckle processorsRoslyn-based (more accurate for complex types)
Config complexitySimpler for basic scenariosMore configuration options
Versioning supportWorks with Asp.VersioningWorks with Asp.Versioning

NSwag key advantage — built-in client generation:

// NSwag Studio or CLI generates typed C# client:
// nswag run nswag.json
// Produces: PortfolioClient.cs with full typed methods

var client = new PortfolioClient(httpClient);
var portfolio = await client.GetPortfolioAsync(42);
// No manual HttpClient calls needed

My preference: Swashbuckle for APIs where I control both ends (simpler setup). NSwag when I need to generate strongly-typed clients for external consumers — saves significant boilerplate.

.NET 9 built-in: Microsoft.AspNetCore.OpenApi is now built into .NET 9, reducing the need for either library for basic scenarios. Scalar replaces Swagger UI with a more modern look.
followUpAnswers.How do you add Swagger for versioned API:
Versioned APIs need one Swagger document per version so consumers can explore each version independently. The key is using AddApiExplorer from the versioning package to make version-aware API discovery work.

// Program.cs — packages: Asp.Versioning.Mvc + Asp.Versioning.Mvc.ApiExplorer + Swashbuckle

builder.Services.AddApiVersioning(options =>
{
    options.DefaultApiVersion = new ApiVersion(1, 0);
    options.AssumeDefaultVersionWhenUnspecified = true;
    options.ReportApiVersions = true;
}).AddApiExplorer(options =>
{
    options.GroupNameFormat = "'v'VVV";         // produces 'v1', 'v2'
    options.SubstituteApiVersionInUrl = true;   // replaces {version} in route templates
});

builder.Services.AddSwaggerGen();

// Configure Swagger docs dynamically from discovered versions
builder.Services.ConfigureOptions<ConfigureSwaggerOptions>();

// ConfigureSwaggerOptions.cs
public class ConfigureSwaggerOptions : IConfigureOptions<SwaggerGenOptions>
{
    private readonly IApiVersionDescriptionProvider _provider;
    public ConfigureSwaggerOptions(IApiVersionDescriptionProvider provider)
        => _provider = provider;

    public void Configure(SwaggerGenOptions options)
    {
        foreach (var description in _provider.ApiVersionDescriptions)
        {
            options.SwaggerDoc(description.GroupName, new OpenApiInfo
            {
                Title = $"My API {description.GroupName}",
                Version = description.GroupName,
                Description = description.IsDeprecated
                    ? "This version is deprecated" : string.Empty
            });
        }
    }
}

// Swagger UI with version dropdown
app.UseSwaggerUI(options =>
{
    foreach (var desc in app.Services
        .GetRequiredService<IApiVersionDescriptionProvider>()
        .ApiVersionDescriptions)
    {
        options.SwaggerEndpoint(
            $"/swagger/{desc.GroupName}/swagger.json",
            desc.GroupName.ToUpperInvariant());
    }
});

questions.tip: Scalar (newer alternative to Swagger UI) is built into .NET 9 and looks much better. If you're on .NET 8, adding Scalar takes 2 lines: builder.Services.AddOpenApi() + app.MapOpenApi(). Worth knowing.

questions.question: What is content negotiation in Web API?
questions.answer:
Content negotiation is the mechanism where the client tells the server what format it wants (via Accept header) and the server selects the best matching format from what it supports.

The client sends: Accept: application/json, application/xml;q=0.8
The server picks the best match and sets Content-Type: application/json in the response.

// ASP.NET Core: add XML formatter alongside JSON
builder.Services.AddControllers()
    .AddXmlSerializerFormatters();

// Restrict what a specific action produces
[Produces("application/json")]
[HttpGet]
public IActionResult Get() { }

[ApiController] default: ASP.NET Core returns JSON by default. If the client requests XML and you haven't added the XML formatter, it still returns JSON (best-effort match). Use [Produces] attribute to hard-code the output format of an action.
followUps: What is the Accept header?
What happens if the server doesn't support the requested format?

followUpAnswers.What is the Accept header?:
The Accept header is sent by the HTTP client to tell the server what media types (MIME types) it can handle in the response. It supports quality values (q) to express preference ordering.

Format: Accept: type/subtype;q=value, type/subtype;q=value

Examples:

// Only JSON
Accept: application/json

// Prefers JSON, accepts XML as fallback (q=0.8 = 80% preference)
Accept: application/json, application/xml;q=0.8

// Accepts anything — no preference
Accept: */*

// Specific content-type negotiation for versioning
Accept: application/vnd.myapi.v2+json  // vendor media type versioning

Related headers:

  • Content-Type — the format the client is sending in the request body (e.g., Content-Type: application/json)
  • Accept-Language — preferred language (e.g., en-US, fr;q=0.9)
  • Accept-Encoding — compression formats the client accepts (e.g., gzip, deflate, br)

In ASP.NET Core: The OutputFormatterSelector reads the Accept header and selects among registered output formatters (JSON, XML, etc.) to serialize the response. The [Produces] attribute overrides client preferences for a specific action.
followUpAnswers.What happens if the server doesn't suppo:
When the client's Accept header requests a format the server doesn't support, ASP.NET Core behavior depends on configuration:

Default behavior (ReturnHttpNotAcceptable = false): Returns the response in the server's default format (JSON) even though the client didn't ask for it. The server ignores the mismatch.

Strict behavior (ReturnHttpNotAcceptable = true): Returns 406 Not Acceptable — the correct HTTP response when no acceptable format is available.

// Enable strict 406 behavior
builder.Services.AddControllers(options =>
{
    options.ReturnHttpNotAcceptable = true;
});

// Now: Accept: application/xml with only JSON registered
// Response: 406 Not Acceptable
// (empty body — no acceptable format to use for the error either)

// Client should retry with a supported format or Accept: */*

406 Not Acceptable in practice:

  • Most browser-originated requests send Accept: */* which always matches — 406 is rare in browser scenarios
  • More common with API clients that explicitly request a specific format (e.g., a legacy system expecting XML)
  • If you support multiple formats, document them clearly in OpenAPI using [Produces("application/json", "application/xml")]

Best practice: Enable ReturnHttpNotAcceptable = true for strict REST compliance. It prevents silent format mismatches that could cause parsing errors on the client side.

questions.tip: Always decorate actions with [Produces] and [Consumes] — this appears in Swagger docs and helps clients know exactly what to send/expect. [ProducesResponseType(StatusCodes.Status200OK, Type = typeof(PortfolioDto))] gives Swagger the response schema.

questions.question: How do you implement rate limiting in ASP.NET Core?
questions.answer:
Rate limiting prevents API abuse by restricting how many requests a client can make in a time window. ASP.NET Core 7+ has built-in rate limiting via Microsoft.AspNetCore.RateLimiting.

// Program.cs
builder.Services.AddRateLimiter(options =>
{
    // Fixed window: 100 requests per minute per IP
    options.AddFixedWindowLimiter("fixed", config =>
    {
        config.PermitLimit = 100;
        config.Window = TimeSpan.FromMinutes(1);
        config.QueueProcessingOrder = QueueProcessingOrder.OldestFirst;
        config.QueueLimit = 5;
    });

    // Sliding window: smoother than fixed (no burst at window reset)
    options.AddSlidingWindowLimiter("api", config =>
    {
        config.PermitLimit = 100;
        config.Window = TimeSpan.FromMinutes(1);
        config.SegmentsPerWindow = 4;  // 4x15s segments
    });

    options.RejectionStatusCode = StatusCodes.Status429TooManyRequests;
});

app.UseRateLimiter();

// Apply to controller
[EnableRateLimiting("api")]
[ApiController]
public class PortfoliosController : ControllerBase { }
followUps: What is a token bucket algorithm?
How do you rate limit per user vs per IP?
What status code should rate limiting return?

followUpAnswers.What is a token bucket algorithm?:
The token bucket algorithm is a rate limiting strategy that allows short bursts of traffic while enforcing an average rate limit over time.

How it works:

  • A bucket holds up to N tokens (burst capacity)
  • Tokens are added at a fixed rate (e.g., 10 per second)
  • Each request consumes one token
  • If the bucket is full, new tokens are discarded
  • If the bucket is empty, the request is rejected or queued

This allows a burst of up to N requests instantly, then sustains at the refill rate.

ASP.NET Core token bucket limiter:

builder.Services.AddRateLimiter(options =>
{
    options.AddTokenBucketLimiter("token-bucket", config =>
    {
        config.TokenLimit = 50;           // max burst: 50 requests
        config.ReplenishmentPeriod = TimeSpan.FromSeconds(10);
        config.TokensPerPeriod = 10;      // +10 tokens every 10 seconds
        config.AutoReplenishment = true;  // background timer refills automatically
        config.QueueLimit = 0;            // reject immediately when empty
    });
});

Algorithm comparison:

AlgorithmBurst allowed?SmoothnessUse case
Fixed windowYes (at window start)Poor (reset spike)Simple quotas
Sliding windowLimitedGoodGeneral APIs
Token bucketYes (controlled)GoodAPIs with bursty clients
Concurrency limiterN/AN/ALimit simultaneous connections
followUpAnswers.How do you rate limit per user vs per IP:
The partition key determines what gets rate limited independently. Using IP rate-limits anonymous clients; using user ID rate-limits authenticated users fairly.

builder.Services.AddRateLimiter(options =>
{
    // Per-IP for anonymous requests
    options.AddPolicy("per-ip", httpContext =>
        RateLimitPartition.GetFixedWindowLimiter(
            partitionKey: httpContext.Connection.RemoteIpAddress?.ToString() ?? "unknown",
            factory: _ => new FixedWindowRateLimiterOptions
            {
                PermitLimit = 30,
                Window = TimeSpan.FromMinutes(1)
            }));

    // Per-user for authenticated requests (higher limit, fairer)
    options.AddPolicy("per-user", httpContext =>
    {
        var userId = httpContext.User.FindFirst(ClaimTypes.NameIdentifier)?.Value;

        // Authenticated: 200 req/min per user
        if (userId != null)
            return RateLimitPartition.GetSlidingWindowLimiter(
                partitionKey: $"user:{userId}",
                factory: _ => new SlidingWindowRateLimiterOptions
                {
                    PermitLimit = 200,
                    Window = TimeSpan.FromMinutes(1),
                    SegmentsPerWindow = 4
                });

        // Unauthenticated: 20 req/min per IP
        return RateLimitPartition.GetFixedWindowLimiter(
            partitionKey: $"anon:{httpContext.Connection.RemoteIpAddress}",
            factory: _ => new FixedWindowRateLimiterOptions
            {
                PermitLimit = 20,
                Window = TimeSpan.FromMinutes(1)
            });
    });

    options.RejectionStatusCode = 429;
});

IP caveat: Users behind NAT (corporate proxy) share an IP — per-IP limits can unfairly block entire offices. For authenticated APIs, always prefer per-user rate limiting.
followUpAnswers.What status code should rate limiting re:
429 Too Many Requests is the correct HTTP status code when a client exceeds their rate limit. It is defined in RFC 6585.

Required/recommended headers with 429:

// ASP.NET Core rate limiting sends these automatically:
HTTP/1.1 429 Too Many Requests
Retry-After: 42          // seconds until the client can retry
X-RateLimit-Limit: 100  // their total limit (optional but helpful)
X-RateLimit-Remaining: 0 // requests remaining in current window
X-RateLimit-Reset: 1716400000  // Unix timestamp when window resets

{
  "type": "https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6585#section-4",
  "title": "Too Many Requests",
  "status": 429,
  "detail": "Rate limit exceeded. Try again in 42 seconds."
}

Setting Retry-After in ASP.NET Core:

builder.Services.AddRateLimiter(options =>
{
    options.RejectionStatusCode = 429;
    options.OnRejected = async (context, ct) =>
    {
        if (context.Lease.TryGetMetadata(
            MetadataName.RetryAfter, out var retryAfter))
        {
            context.HttpContext.Response.Headers.RetryAfter =
                ((int)retryAfter.TotalSeconds).ToString();
        }
        context.HttpContext.Response.StatusCode = 429;
        await context.HttpContext.Response.WriteAsJsonAsync(
            new ProblemDetails
            {
                Status = 429,
                Title = "Rate limit exceeded",
                Detail = retryAfter.HasValue
                    ? $"Retry after {retryAfter.Value.TotalSeconds:0} seconds"
                    : "Too many requests"
            }, ct);
    };
});

Do NOT return 503 Service Unavailable — that implies the server is down, not that the client is being throttled. 429 clearly communicates the client is at fault.

questions.tip: 429 Too Many Requests is the correct status code for rate limiting. Include a Retry-After header telling the client when they can try again. For public APIs, use IP-based limits; for authenticated APIs, use user-based limits to be fair.

questions.question: What is the IActionResult return type vs specific types like Ok(), NotFound()?
questions.answer:
ASP.NET Core gives you several options for returning from an action:

1. IActionResult — most flexible:

public async Task<IActionResult> Get(int id)
{
    var item = await _service.GetAsync(id);
    if (item == null) return NotFound();     // 404
    return Ok(item);                          // 200 + JSON
    // or: return Created(url, item);         // 201
    // or: return NoContent();                // 204
}

2. ActionResult<T> — strongly typed with Swagger benefits:

// Swagger knows return type is Portfolio
public async Task<ActionResult<Portfolio>> Get(int id)
{
    var item = await _service.GetAsync(id);
    return item == null ? NotFound() : item;  // implicit conversion
}

3. Direct type return:

public async Task<Portfolio> Get(int id) { ... }

Returns 200 always — can't return 404, 400 etc. Avoid in APIs.

My preference: ActionResult<T> — gives type safety for Swagger while still allowing different status codes.
followUps: What is Results vs IActionResult in minimal APIs?
How does [ProducesResponseType] help with Swagger?

followUpAnswers.What is Results vs IActionResult in mini:
In minimal APIs, Results (static class) replaces the controller helper methods like Ok(), NotFound(). It's the minimal API equivalent of returning IActionResult.

Controller API (IActionResult):

// Controller action
[HttpGet("{id}")]
public async Task<IActionResult> Get(int id)
{
    var item = await _svc.GetAsync(id);
    return item == null ? NotFound() : Ok(item);
}

Minimal API (Results / IResult):

// Minimal API endpoint
app.MapGet("/api/portfolios/{id:int}", async (int id, IPortfolioService svc) =>
{
    var item = await svc.GetAsync(id);
    return item == null
        ? Results.NotFound()
        : Results.Ok(item);
});

// Or use TypedResults for Swagger schema inference (recommended)
app.MapGet("/api/portfolios/{id:int}",
    async Task<Results<Ok<Portfolio>, NotFound>>
    (int id, IPortfolioService svc) =>
{
    var item = await svc.GetAsync(id);
    return item == null
        ? TypedResults.NotFound()
        : TypedResults.Ok(item);
});

TypedResults vs Results: TypedResults (the static class returning strongly-typed Results<T1,T2> union types) allows Swagger to infer the response schema without [ProducesResponseType]. Always prefer TypedResults in minimal APIs for automatic OpenAPI integration.
followUpAnswers.How does [ProducesResponseType] help wit:
[ProducesResponseType] is an attribute that tells Swagger the possible HTTP status codes and response body types an action can return. Without it, Swagger can only infer the 200 response from ActionResult<T>; it can't know about 404, 400, or 201 responses.

[HttpGet("{id:int}")]
[ProducesResponseType(typeof(PortfolioDto), StatusCodes.Status200OK)]
[ProducesResponseType(StatusCodes.Status404NotFound)]
[ProducesResponseType(StatusCodes.Status401Unauthorized)]
public async Task<IActionResult> Get(int id)
{
    var portfolio = await _svc.GetAsync(id);
    return portfolio == null ? NotFound() : Ok(portfolio);
}

// POST — multiple response types
[HttpPost]
[ProducesResponseType(typeof(PortfolioDto), StatusCodes.Status201Created)]
[ProducesResponseType(typeof(ValidationProblemDetails), StatusCodes.Status400BadRequest)]
[ProducesResponseType(StatusCodes.Status409Conflict)]
public async Task<IActionResult> Create(CreatePortfolioRequest req) { }

What this generates in Swagger:

  • Each status code appears as a separate response tab in Swagger UI
  • The response schema is shown for each code that has a type
  • Client code generators (NSwag, AutoRest) produce the correct return types

Cleaner alternative — use ActionResult<T>:

// ActionResult<T> tells Swagger the 200 type automatically
// Still need [ProducesResponseType] for non-200 status codes
public async Task<ActionResult<PortfolioDto>> Get(int id)
{
    // Swagger auto-infers 200 returns PortfolioDto
    // Still add [ProducesResponseType(404)] for completeness
}

questions.tip: Use ActionResult<T> over IActionResult whenever you have a specific return type. Swagger will correctly generate the response schema. With plain IActionResult, Swagger can't infer the response type unless you add [ProducesResponseType] attributes.

questions.question: What is minimal API in ASP.NET Core and when would you use it over controllers?
questions.answer:
Minimal APIs (introduced in .NET 6) define HTTP endpoints directly in Program.cs without controllers, using lambda-based handlers:

// Minimal API
app.MapGet("/api/portfolios/{id:int}", async (int id, IPortfolioService svc) =>
{
    var portfolio = await svc.GetAsync(id);
    return portfolio == null ? Results.NotFound() : Results.Ok(portfolio);
});

app.MapPost("/api/portfolios", async (CreatePortfolioRequest req, IPortfolioService svc) =>
{
    var created = await svc.CreateAsync(req);
    return Results.Created($"/api/portfolios/{created.Id}", created);
});

When to use Minimal API:

  • Microservices with few endpoints — low ceremony, fast startup, better performance
  • Azure Functions-style compute services
  • Prototyping / simple webhooks

When to stick with controllers:

  • Complex APIs with many actions — grouping by controller is cleaner
  • Need action filters extensively (model validation, auth, caching)
  • Team familiar with MVC pattern

In the CS_PM_Service microservices project I'd use minimal APIs for the lightweight event processors but keep controllers for the complex management endpoints.
followUps: How do you add middleware/filters to minimal APIs?
How does model validation work in minimal APIs?

followUpAnswers.How do you add middleware/filters to min:
Minimal APIs support endpoint filters (the equivalent of action filters) and standard middleware. The API is different from controller filters but achieves the same goals.

Endpoint filters (minimal API equivalent of action filters):

// Define a filter
public class TimingFilter : IEndpointFilter
{
    public async ValueTask<object?> InvokeAsync(
        EndpointFilterInvocationContext context,
        EndpointFilterDelegate next)
    {
        var sw = Stopwatch.StartNew();
        var result = await next(context);  // execute the endpoint
        sw.Stop();
        Console.WriteLine($"Request took {sw.ElapsedMilliseconds}ms");
        return result;
    }
}

// Apply to a single endpoint
app.MapGet("/api/portfolios", GetPortfolios)
   .AddEndpointFilter<TimingFilter>();

// Apply to a group of endpoints
var group = app.MapGroup("/api/portfolios")
    .AddEndpointFilter<TimingFilter>()
    .RequireAuthorization();  // applies [Authorize] to all in group

group.MapGet("/", GetAll);
group.MapGet("/{id:int}", GetById);
group.MapPost("/", Create);

Short-circuit from a filter:

public async ValueTask<object?> InvokeAsync(
    EndpointFilterInvocationContext ctx, EndpointFilterDelegate next)
{
    // Validate an API key header
    if (!ctx.HttpContext.Request.Headers.TryGetValue("X-Api-Key", out var key)
        || key != "expected-key")
        return Results.Unauthorized();  // short-circuit

    return await next(ctx);  // proceed
}

Global middleware still applies to minimal API endpoints exactly as in controller-based APIs — app.UseAuthentication(), app.UseAuthorization(), app.UseCors() all work the same way.
followUpAnswers.How does model validation work in minima:
Minimal APIs do NOT automatically validate models like [ApiController] does. You must handle validation explicitly — this is a key difference from controller APIs.

Option 1 — Manual validation with data annotations:

app.MapPost("/api/portfolios", async (
    CreatePortfolioRequest req,
    IValidator<CreatePortfolioRequest> validator) =>
{
    // Manual validation needed — no auto-400
    var validation = await validator.ValidateAsync(req);
    if (!validation.IsValid)
        return Results.ValidationProblem(validation.ToDictionary());

    // proceed...
});

Option 2 — Validation filter (reusable across endpoints):

public class ValidationFilter<T> : IEndpointFilter
    where T : class
{
    private readonly IValidator<T> _validator;
    public ValidationFilter(IValidator<T> validator) => _validator = validator;

    public async ValueTask<object?> InvokeAsync(
        EndpointFilterInvocationContext ctx, EndpointFilterDelegate next)
    {
        // Find the T argument in the endpoint parameters
        var arg = ctx.Arguments.OfType<T>().FirstOrDefault();
        if (arg != null)
        {
            var result = await _validator.ValidateAsync(arg);
            if (!result.IsValid)
                return Results.ValidationProblem(result.ToDictionary());
        }
        return await next(ctx);
    }
}

// Use it cleanly
app.MapPost("/api/portfolios", Create)
   .AddEndpointFilter<ValidationFilter<CreatePortfolioRequest>>();

.NET 9 — built-in validation: .NET 9 added WithParameterValidation() which auto-validates data annotations on minimal API parameters — closing the gap with controller APIs. Available via app.MapPost(...).WithParameterValidation().

questions.tip: Minimal APIs and controllers can coexist in the same app. Use controllers for your complex CRUD endpoints and minimal APIs for simple webhook receivers or health check endpoints. The performance difference matters only at extreme scale.
